inline_review:
commit 34a2c54d87fec0fc08038fb4b1cf7c1bb3b5c1c6
Author: Muchun Song <muchun.song@linux.dev>
Subject: mm/sparse: fix BUILD_BUG_ON check for section map alignment

This commit updates the compile-time check for section map alignment to
accurately reflect the mathematically guaranteed alignment. It calculates
the available bits using PFN_SECTION_SHIFT + __ffs(sizeof(struct page))
rather than just PFN_SECTION_SHIFT.

> diff --git a/mm/sparse.c b/mm/sparse.c
> --- a/mm/sparse.c
> +++ b/mm/sparse.c
> @@ -269,7 +269,8 @@ static unsigned long sparse_encode_mem_map(struct page *mem_map, unsigned long p
>  {
>  	unsigned long coded_mem_map =
>  		(unsigned long)(mem_map - (section_nr_to_pfn(pnum)));
> -	BUILD_BUG_ON(SECTION_MAP_LAST_BIT > PFN_SECTION_SHIFT);
> +	BUILD_BUG_ON(SECTION_MAP_LAST_BIT > min(PFN_SECTION_SHIFT + __ffs(sizeof(struct page)),
> +						PAGE_SHIFT));

Can this cause a build failure on architectures like csky, m68k, and alpha?

On these architectures, __ffs() is defined as an inline function that
unconditionally executes inline assembly, without a __builtin_constant_p()
fallback. Since compilers treat inline assembly as opaque, they cannot
constant-fold it even when the input is a known constant like
sizeof(struct page). This prevents the BUILD_BUG_ON() condition from being
evaluated statically.

Would it be better to use a compile-time safe alternative like
__builtin_ctzl() instead?

Also, could the use of min() here cause issues since it expands into a
statement expression, which is generally avoided in compile-time
constraints?

>  	BUG_ON(coded_mem_map & ~SECTION_MAP_MASK);
>  	return coded_mem_map;
>  }

Target Commit:
commit 34a2c54d87fec0fc08038fb4b1cf7c1bb3b5c1c6
Author: Muchun Song <muchun.song@linux.dev>
Date:   Tue Mar 31 11:30:23 2026 +0000

    mm/sparse: fix BUILD_BUG_ON check for section map alignment
    
    The comment in mmzone.h states that the alignment requirement
    is the minimum of PAGE_SHIFT and PFN_SECTION_SHIFT. However, the
    pointer arithmetic (mem_map - section_nr_to_pfn()) results in
    a byte offset scaled by sizeof(struct page). Thus, the actual
    alignment provided by the second term is PFN_SECTION_SHIFT +
    __ffs(sizeof(struct page)).
    
    Update the compile-time check and the mmzone.h comment to
    accurately reflect this mathematically guaranteed alignment by
    taking the minimum of PAGE_SHIFT and PFN_SECTION_SHIFT +
    __ffs(sizeof(struct page)). This avoids the issue of the check
    being overly restrictive on architectures like powerpc where
    PFN_SECTION_SHIFT alone is very small (e.g., 6).
    
    Also, remove the exhaustive per-architecture bit-width list from the
    comment; such details risk falling out of date over time and may
    inadvertently be left un-updated, while the existing BUILD_BUG_ON
    provides sufficient compile-time verification of the constraint.
    
    No runtime impact so far: SECTION_MAP_LAST_BIT happens to fit within
    the smaller limit on all existing architectures.
    
    Fixes: def9b71ee651 ("include/linux/mmzone.h: fix explanation of lower bits in the SPARSEMEM mem_map pointer")
    Signed-off-by: Muchun Song <songmuchun@bytedance.com>

diff --git a/include/linux/mmzone.h b/include/linux/mmzone.h
index 3e51190a55e4..bacb49ba5aeb 100644
--- a/include/linux/mmzone.h
+++ b/include/linux/mmzone.h
@@ -1981,21 +1981,15 @@ static inline struct mem_section *__nr_to_section(unsigned long nr)
 extern size_t mem_section_usage_size(void);
 
 /*
- * We use the lower bits of the mem_map pointer to store
- * a little bit of information.  The pointer is calculated
- * as mem_map - section_nr_to_pfn(pnum).  The result is
- * aligned to the minimum alignment of the two values:
- *   1. All mem_map arrays are page-aligned.
- *   2. section_nr_to_pfn() always clears PFN_SECTION_SHIFT
- *      lowest bits.  PFN_SECTION_SHIFT is arch-specific
- *      (equal SECTION_SIZE_BITS - PAGE_SHIFT), and the
- *      worst combination is powerpc with 256k pages,
- *      which results in PFN_SECTION_SHIFT equal 6.
- * To sum it up, at least 6 bits are available on all architectures.
- * However, we can exceed 6 bits on some other architectures except
- * powerpc (e.g. 15 bits are available on x86_64, 13 bits are available
- * with the worst case of 64K pages on arm64) if we make sure the
- * exceeded bit is not applicable to powerpc.
+ * We use the lower bits of the mem_map pointer to store a little bit of
+ * information. The pointer is calculated as mem_map - section_nr_to_pfn().
+ * The result is aligned to the minimum alignment of the two values:
+ *
+ * 1. All mem_map arrays are page-aligned.
+ * 2. section_nr_to_pfn() always clears PFN_SECTION_SHIFT lowest bits. Because
+ *    it is subtracted from a struct page pointer, the offset is scaled by
+ *    sizeof(struct page). This provides an alignment of PFN_SECTION_SHIFT +
+ *    __ffs(sizeof(struct page)).
  */
 enum {
 	SECTION_MARKED_PRESENT_BIT,
diff --git a/mm/sparse.c b/mm/sparse.c
index b5b2b6f7041b..0da31f7ee8b6 100644
--- a/mm/sparse.c
+++ b/mm/sparse.c
@@ -269,7 +269,8 @@ static unsigned long sparse_encode_mem_map(struct page *mem_map, unsigned long p
 {
 	unsigned long coded_mem_map =
 		(unsigned long)(mem_map - (section_nr_to_pfn(pnum)));
-	BUILD_BUG_ON(SECTION_MAP_LAST_BIT > PFN_SECTION_SHIFT);
+	BUILD_BUG_ON(SECTION_MAP_LAST_BIT > min(PFN_SECTION_SHIFT + __ffs(sizeof(struct page)),
+						PAGE_SHIFT));
 	BUG_ON(coded_mem_map & ~SECTION_MAP_MASK);
 	return coded_mem_map;
 }

    "thought": "**Analyzing the Sparse Memory Map Alignment Calculation**

Okay, so I'm diving into this commit that touches `mm/sparse.c` and `include/linux/mmzone.h`, specifically dealing with a `BUILD_BUG_ON` check and a comment about `mem_map` pointer alignment. My initial thought was: the comment about the alignment being the minimum of `PAGE_SHIFT` and `PFN_SECTION_SHIFT` didn't quite gel with my understanding of the pointer arithmetic involved.

I had to refresh my knowledge of the sparse memory map code. `mem_map` is a `struct page *`, and the `section_nr_to_pfn()` function gives us an `unsigned long` PFN. The original calculation, as represented in `sparse_encode_mem_map()`, subtracts `section_nr_to_pfn(pnum)` from `mem_map`. Initially, I thought this was simple address calculation, like subtracting an offset, but it's more complicated because of the `struct page` involved.

The core of the matter is the pointer arithmetic itself. Subtracting a PFN from `mem_map` is effectively subtracting the number of `struct page` elements represented by that PFN. Since the result of the subtraction is cast to an `unsigned long`, what we're actually looking at is the *virtual address* represented by this calculation. So `coded_mem_map` is `mem_map`'s virtual address minus the `section_nr_to_pfn` value scaled by `sizeof(struct page)`.

The alignment of the resulting `coded_mem_map` is determined by the alignments of its components. `mem_map` is page-aligned, so it has `PAGE_SHIFT` trailing zeros. `section_nr_to_pfn(pnum)` is `pnum << PFN_SECTION_SHIFT`, which means it has `PFN_SECTION_SHIFT` trailing zeros. When we multiply by `sizeof(struct page)`, we need to take into account the trailing zeros of `sizeof(struct page)`. So, the multiplication introduces an additional `__ffs(sizeof(struct page))` zeros. This means that after the multiplication, `section_nr_to_pfn(pnum) * sizeof(struct page)` has `PFN_SECTION_SHIFT + __ffs(sizeof(struct page))` trailing zeros. The difference will therefore have `min(PAGE_SHIFT, PFN_SECTION_SHIFT + __ffs(sizeof(struct page)))` trailing zeros.

The crucial point became whether `__ffs` is usable within a `BUILD_BUG_ON`.  That's where things got interesting. `BUILD_BUG_ON` needs a compile-time constant expression. My initial thought was that `__ffs` might be an inline function with assembly instructions, and those are generally not compile-time constants.

Looking at the code, it's clear that `__ffs` is often implemented as a built-in or inline assembly. I found an example using `asm` in x86. This confirmed my suspicion that it wouldn't be reliably usable within a `BUILD_BUG_ON`, because it's *not* a constant in those cases. The `BUILD_BUG_ON` condition would then trigger a compile error on architectures where it's an inline assembly, precisely as the author indicated.

I then started thinking about alternatives and potential definitions and usage. Alternatives could be `__builtin_ctz`, or `ilog2` for powers of two. But `sizeof(struct page)` is not always guaranteed to be a power of two. In fact, if `sizeof(struct page)` is 56 bytes, then the first bit is 3 (i.e. `__ffs(56)` is 3). I searched for similar usage in `BUILD_BUG_ON`. Typically, `ilog2()` or `__builtin_ctz()` or even bit manipulations on the `sizeof(struct page)` were used in those cases. My current investigation involves reading `arch/x86/include/asm/bitops.h` and `include/asm-generic/bitops/__ffs.h` to see how `__ffs` is defined, especially if it utilizes `__builtin_constant_p`.

    "thought": "**My Immediate Thoughts on `__ffs()` and Compile-Time Evaluation**

Okay, so `__ffs()` – that's almost always an inline function or macro, frequently dipping into inline assembly for efficiency. I know that using it within a `BUILD_BUG_ON()` statement is a common trick to force compile-time evaluation. It's a handy technique for detecting issues early in the build process, right?

But wait a minute... If `__ffs()` is using inline assembly *without* a `__builtin_constant_p()` check to ensure it's dealing with constants, the compiler can't reliably evaluate it as a constant expression. That means `BUILD_BUG_ON()` will, in fact, *fail* to compile, and in a way that is less than helpful to the developer in identifying the error. This is a crucial detail I need to remember. I'm going to make a mental note here to always be aware of the interaction between inline assembly, compile-time expressions, and error checking with `BUILD_BUG_ON()`.

Let me just quickly check `include/asm-generic/bitops/__ffs.h` to make sure I'm remembering this right...

    "thought": "**My Deep Dive into `BUILD_BUG_ON` and Compile-Time Assertions**

Okay, so I'm digging into how `BUILD_BUG_ON` works, and I've got a bit of a mental tangle I need to sort out. It *looks* like `__compiletime_assert` relies on an `extern void prefix ## suffix(void) __compiletime_error(msg);` function, which gets called if the condition is false. The neat trick is that the optimizer *should* see this as dead code if the condition's always true and eliminate the call, right?

But, hold on, it's not quite that simple. `BUILD_BUG_ON` doesn't directly use `compiletime_assert` *in all* its forms for error generation. It's actually a macro that expands to `BUILD_BUG_ON_MSG`, which in turn, *does* call `compiletime_assert`. So far, so good, it still relies on the optimizer eliminating the function call.

Now, the crucial question becomes, does the optimizer *really* treat this whole expression as a constant? I'm looking at something like `min(PFN_SECTION_SHIFT + __ffs(sizeof(struct page)), PAGE_SHIFT)`. `__ffs` is inline, so the optimizer *should* be able to inline it, and evaluate the built-in intrinsic, or the inline assembly.

Here's the rub! If `__ffs` uses inline assembly, like `rep; bsf` or `tzcnt` on x86, or `ct0` on Hexagon, the optimizer can't *always* evaluate the inline assembly at compile time. That's a deal-breaker! If it can't evaluate the inline assembly, it can't prove that the condition `SECTION_MAP_LAST_BIT > ...` is false. The call to `__compiletime_error` stays, and the build breaks with a link error or a compile-time error.

I need to trace this more closely for the `__ffs` implementation on x86 specifically. Let me check the definition! Ah, there it is:

```c
#define __ffs(word)				\
	(__builtin_constant_p(word) ?		\
	 (unsigned long)__builtin_ctzl(word) :	\
	 variable__ffs(word))
```

Ah, brilliant! Since `sizeof(struct page)` *is* a constant, `__builtin_constant_p` evaluates to true and it uses the `__builtin_ctzl()`. The optimizer can easily evaluate that and eliminate the call. Okay, X86 is safe.

Now, let's look at ARM64. It's just a simple:

```c
static __always_inline __attribute_const__ unsigned int __ffs(unsigned long word)
{
	return __builtin_ctzl(word);
}
```
Yeah, that's just a direct call to the intrinsic. ARM64 is fine as well.

Let me check ARM (32-bit) in `arch/arm/include/asm/bitops.h` and see what's going on there. I'll need to check if there are any gotchas in that specific architecture.

The core of my concern here is ensuring this whole expression resolves cleanly, especially during compilation. If it's all constant, no problem, standard stuff. But if `__ffs` *isn't* completely resolvable at compile time... that's where things get interesting.

We already considered the case where even as an inline function, it's inside an `if()` statement, and GCC generally optimizes that away, right? Right, dead-code elimination. But what about architectures that are using *custom* inline assembly for `__ffs`? That's the key question, isn't it? If `__ffs` *is* an inline function, GCC sees it, parses it, and uses the compile-time-computable `__builtin_ctzl`, and optimizes it away. That’s probably the standard case, but I have to be sure. Okay, so I'm looking at this patch and, initially, the `__ffs` usage caught my eye.  It's used *everywhere*, even indirectly within `BUILD_BUG_ON` macros, like in that `GENMASK` example. That means, thankfully, that the compiler treats it as a compile-time constant, which is what we need to get reliable compile-time evaluation.  Thus, `PFN_SECTION_SHIFT + __ffs(sizeof(struct page))` will indeed be a compile-time constant.  Given how the `min` macro works, that's fine for `BUILD_BUG_ON`, no issues there.

But then I started thinking about the relationship between `PFN_SECTION_SHIFT` and `PAGE_SHIFT`. Could `PFN_SECTION_SHIFT` be *larger* than `PAGE_SHIFT`? Let's check: it's defined as `SECTION_SIZE_BITS - PAGE_SHIFT`. Okay, if `SECTION_SIZE_BITS` is 27 (128MB) and `PAGE_SHIFT` is 12 (4KB), then `PFN_SECTION_SHIFT` is 15.  Yep, 15 *is* larger than 12.  So, `min(15 + 6, 12)` evaluates to 12. And that means the `BUILD_BUG_ON` condition will pass because `SECTION_MAP_LAST_BIT` (which is 6) is <= 12.

Let's consider another example, with 64KB pages on PowerPC: `SECTION_SIZE_BITS` = 24 and `PAGE_SHIFT` = 16.  `PFN_SECTION_SHIFT` becomes 8. If `sizeof(struct page)` is 64,  `__ffs` is 6.  8 + 6 = 14, and `min(14, 16)` is 14.  Again,  `SECTION_MAP_LAST_BIT` (6) is <= 14, so it still passes.

Then, with 256K pages on PowerPC, let's say `PAGE_SHIFT` is 18. And `SECTION_SIZE_BITS` is 24. `PFN_SECTION_SHIFT` = 6.  `6 + 6 = 12`, so `min(12, 18)` is 12.  `SECTION_MAP_LAST_BIT` (6) is <= 12, still passing.

Wait a second... Is `SECTION_SIZE_BITS` *always* strictly greater than `PAGE_SHIFT`?  Yes, logically a section is a chunk of memory that contains *multiple* pages.  `SECTION_SIZE_BITS` is usually at least 24 (16MB) up to 30 (1GB).  Therefore, `PFN_SECTION_SHIFT` is always at least 6. If `sizeof(struct page)` is 32 bytes (`__ffs` is 5). Then, `PFN_SECTION_SHIFT + 5` is at least 11.  `PAGE_SHIFT` is at least 12. So `min(...)` is always going to be at least 11. `SECTION_MAP_LAST_BIT` is 6.  `6 <= 11` will *always* be true, so this compile-time check will *never* fail under those conditions.

Wait, are there ANY issues with this patch, then? Let's re-examine the patch description and diff. It's about fixing the check `BUILD_BUG_ON(SECTION_MAP_LAST_BIT > PFN_SECTION_SHIFT)`. Ah! The original check was *overly restrictive*.

With 256K pages on powerpc: `PFN_SECTION_SHIFT` = 6.

The key question pops up: is the result of that call to `pfn_to_page` guaranteed to be page-aligned?

I know how `pfn_to_page` works under the hood. In the `vmemmap` case, it's just an offset calculation: `vmemmap + (pfn)`. `vmemmap` itself is heavily aligned, typically to a PMD boundary or at least a page. So, the result of  `pfn_to_page(section_nr_to_pfn(sec))` becomes `vmemmap + sec * PAGES_PER_SECTION`.  `sec * PAGES_PER_SECTION` then turns into `sec << (SECTION_SIZE_BITS - PAGE_SHIFT)`.

But, wait a second, is  `PAGES_PER_SECTION * sizeof(struct page)` always a multiple of `PAGE_SIZE`? Let's check.  `PAGES_PER_SECTION` is calculated as `2^(SECTION_SIZE_BITS - PAGE_SHIFT)`.

I can see some examples, where the math works out fine, like on a 4KB page size with a 2MB section size, we have `32768 * 64 = 2MB`.  The issue is that in some cases, the section size can be smaller, for example, on powerpc, `SECTION_SIZE_BITS` = 24 and `PAGE_SHIFT` = 18 and `PAGES_PER_SECTION` = `2^6 = 64`.

Here is the problem! It becomes `64 * 64 = 4096`. However, in this configuration, `PAGE_SIZE` is 256KB!  The memmap is only 4KB long, which is not aligned to the page size.

A natively compile-time safe alternative like `__builtin_ctzl()` must be used instead. Okay, so I've got this build failure popping up on multiple architectures, and it seems to stem from using `__ffs()` inside a `BUILD_BUG_ON()` condition. Let's break this down. On x86, `__ffs()` has a fallback that uses `__builtin_ctzl` when it can be evaluated at compile time, which works. However, architectures like `alpha`, `csky`, and `m68k` are unconditionally using inline assembly in their `__ffs()` implementations. This is the crux of the problem.

`BUILD_BUG_ON()` relies on the compiler's dead-code elimination to statically determine if a condition is constant at compile time and discard the error path if it is. When there's inline assembly, the compiler can't "see" what it does, so it doesn't know the result at compile time, and it has to treat it as a runtime value. Because of this, the compiler cannot eliminate the `__compiletime_error` function call and the build fails.

I'm seeing that the patch uses `__ffs(sizeof(struct page))` inside `BUILD_BUG_ON()`. Now, `__ffs()` isn't constant on those architectures, since it uses inline assembly, making the `BUILD_BUG_ON()` fail. So, the fix is to replace `__ffs()` with something the compiler *can* evaluate at compile time. Something like `__builtin_ctzl(sizeof(struct page))` should do the trick.

Then there's the `min()` macro within the `BUILD_BUG_ON()`. That's a statement expression, which isn't ideal for these compile-time checks, but it's not the primary cause of the breakage, so I can include it. But I'm going to note that using the ternary operator `(A < B ? A : B)` is the preferred approach for this kind of thing.

Since this is a build issue, it's a Low severity issue. This is explicitly stated in the documentation.

This prevents the compiler from constant-folding the condition. The solution is to use a compile-time safe alternative like `__builtin_ctzl`. Author: Muchun Song <muchun.song@linux.dev>
Subject: mm/sparse: fix BUILD_BUG_ON check for section map alignment

This commit updates the compile-time check for section map alignment to
accurately reflect the mathematically guaranteed alignment. It calculates
the available bits using PFN_SECTION_SHIFT + __ffs(sizeof(struct page))
rather than just PFN_SECTION_SHIFT.

> diff --git a/mm/sparse.c b/mm/sparse.c
> --- a/mm/sparse.c
> +++ b/mm/sparse.c
> @@ -269,7 +269,8 @@ static unsigned long sparse_encode_mem_map(struct page *mem_map, unsigned long p
>  {
>  	unsigned long coded_mem_map =
>  		(unsigned long)(mem_map - (section_nr_to_pfn(pnum)));
> -	BUILD_BUG_ON(SECTION_MAP_LAST_BIT > PFN_SECTION_SHIFT);
> +	BUILD_BUG_ON(SECTION_MAP_LAST_BIT > min(PFN_SECTION_SHIFT + __ffs(sizeof(struct page)),
> +						PAGE_SHIFT));

Can this cause a build failure on architectures like csky, m68k, and alpha?

On these architectures, __ffs() is defined as an inline function that
unconditionally executes inline assembly, without a __builtin_constant_p()
fallback. Since compilers treat inline assembly as opaque, they cannot
constant-fold it even when the input is a known constant like
sizeof(struct page). This prevents the BUILD_BUG_ON() condition from being
evaluated statically.

Would it be better to use a compile-time safe alternative like
__builtin_ctzl() instead?

Also, could the use of min() here cause issues since it expands into a
statement expression, which is generally avoided in compile-time
constraints?

>  	BUG_ON(coded_mem_map & ~SECTION_MAP_MASK);
>  	return coded_mem_map;
>  }

",
